title: Anti-Churn Pricing Design

content.statement: Pricing should reduce regret after purchase.
content.question: Does a member who used your app 0 times this month feel guilty, angry, or OK with the charge?
content.equation: ACPD = (Value Consistency × Usage Fit × Predictability × Trust) ÷ Regret
sop: Identify your recurring charge or subscription price point.
Rate it 1-10 on Value Consistency, does the customer get roughly equal value every cycle, or does it vary wildly (see calculation, page 6).
Rate it 1-10 on Usage Fit and Predictability, does the price match how much they actually use it, and do they know what's coming (see calculation, page 6).
Rate it 1-10 on Trust, and rate Regret 1-10 for how often customers feel the charge wasn't worth it (see calculation, page 6).
If Regret is high, redesign the offer around usage (pause options, tiered plans, usage reminders) rather than just improving the product.
content.explainer: The charge that feels reasonable stays; the one that feels painful churns.
questions: Does a member who used your app 0 times this month feel guilty, angry, or OK with the charge?
If a customer forgot they were even paying you, would that feel like loyalty or like a ticking time bomb?
What's the exact moment in a billing cycle when a customer decides whether this charge was worth it?

definitions.def: The rate at which customers stop paying or stop using a product.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Value Consistency 7, Usage Fit 6, Predictability 8, Trust 7. 7 times 6 times 8 times 7 equals 2,352, divided by Regret 3 equals 784. Higher scores mean the charge feels earned every time it hits. Lower scores mean customers are quietly building a case to cancel.
content.closing_statement: A charge only survives if it still feels earned.

caption:
Principle Nº 66: Anti-Churn Pricing Design

Pricing should reduce regret after purchase. The charge that feels reasonable stays; the one that feels painful churns.

Ask yourself: Does a member who used your app 0 times this month feel guilty, angry, or OK with the charge?
